brief:
The single most striking feature of this week's landscape is the absolute stillness — not a single basis point moved across the entire sovereign curve, from the 91-day bill at **9.85%** all the way out to the 15-year bond at **12.68%**. In a market where inflation sits at **9.13%** and the repo rate is anchored at **10%**, that kind of frozen pricing is not tranquility, it is indecision masquerading as equilibrium. The curve itself tells a deeply contradictory story: a well-behaved front end rising from 91-day at 9.85% through 182-day at 10.2%, then an inversion at the 364-day tenor which drops to **10.07%** — thirteen basis points below the six-month bill — before climbing through the 5-year at 10.82% and 10-year at 11.15%, only to see the 2-year at **11.95%** printing a stunning 80 basis points above the 10-year. The 15-year at **12.68%** towers over the 20-year at 11.65% by over a hundred basis points. These are not normal curve dynamics. The 10Y-91D spread of **130 basis points** looks reasonable in isolation, but it conceals severe kinks that suggest fragmented demand rather than coherent rate expectations. The March 10th T-bill auctions cleared cleanly — the 91-day at a healthy bid-cover of **1.92x** and the 364-day at **1.75x**, both with zero devolvement — while the 10-year BGTB auction on March 8th managed only **1.4x** coverage and forced **10% devolvement** on Bangladesh Bank's books. That devolvement is the real signal: the market is comfortable lending the government money for a year, but balks at a decade.

The structural explanation for this curve shape lies at the intersection of excess liquidity and fiscal stress. The system is swimming in ৳**1.9 lakh crore** of excess liquidity, and call money is trading at **9.8%** — just 20 basis points below repo — which tells you banks are parking cash rather than deploying it aggressively. The ADR at **75.8%** confirms the same story: private credit appetite is tepid, so banks are natural buyers of short-duration government paper, which explains the strong bid-covers on T-bills. But the reluctance to extend duration into 10-year and beyond reflects a deeper concern. Revenue collection at just **44.9%** of FY26 target with only months remaining, ADP implementation at a dismal **11.5%**, and an interest-to-revenue ratio of **22%** collectively paint a picture of a sovereign that will need to borrow more, not less, and will likely do so at longer tenors to avoid bunching maturities. The market is pricing that supply risk into the belly and the long end. The bizarre 2-year at 11.95% — sitting above the 5-year, 10-year, and 20-year — almost certainly reflects either thin secondary market activity or a single poorly-priced auction that has become the reference point. If real, it represents a mispricing that will correct; if it is stale data, it is misleading our curve analytics. Either way, it demands investigation before we position around it. Meanwhile, inflation remains sticky with food CPI at **9.3%**, reserves are holding at ৳**35.1 billion** with the taka at **121.2**, and Brent at **$78.4** and JKM LNG at **$12.8/mmbtu** are neither helpful nor threatening — they are neutral enough to keep BB on the sidelines for now.

My recommendation to this committee is threefold in spirit. First, the front end is fully valued but safe — the 91-day at 9.85% offers negative real yield against 9.13% CPI, but it is liquid, carries zero devolvement risk, and sits comfortably within BB's corridor. We should maintain our SLR buffers here but not add aggressively. Second, the real opportunity lives in the **5-year tenor at 10.82%** — it cleared at 1.6x coverage with no devolvement, offers roughly 170 basis points of real yield, and sits at the sweet spot of the curve before the kinks distort value. If we believe BB holds repo at 10% through FY26 — and I do, given sticky inflation and the fiscal revenue gap — then locking in 5-year paper at 10.82% offers genuine carry. The danger zone is the 10-year and beyond, where devolvement risk is live and the government's borrowing trajectory will only steepen as the fiscal year draws to a close. I would avoid adding 10-year or 15-year exposure until bid-covers improve above 1.6x and devolvement clears to zero. The catalyst to watch is the next BGTB auction cycle: if devolvement on the 10-year persists or worsens, it will signal that the market is demanding a concession that BB is unwilling to give, and that tension eventually breaks toward higher long-end yields — which means mark-to-market pain for anyone overweight duration today.
